data.title: Chinese online retailers retreat as Trump ends de minimis exemption
data.description:
Shares of PDD Holdings (NASDAQ:PDD), the parent company of online marketplace Temu, JD.com (JD), and Alibaba (BABA) fell sharply in late trading after President Trump confirmed he was eliminating the de minimis treatment for low-value imports from China through an Executive Order.

According to the White House, U.S. Customs and Border Protection processes over 4 million de minimis shipments into the U.S. each day.

The de minimis exemption allows goods with a value of $800 or less to enter the U.S. duty-free. Temu, SHEIN, AliExpress and other Chinese retailers exploit this exemption to ship individual packages to customers out of the country without paying import tariffs and precludes these retailers from operating fulfillment centers in the U.S. similar to Amazon (AMZN) and Walmart (WMT).

The order will take effect on May 2 at 12:01am EDT.

The Executive Order states that imported goods sent through means other than the international postal network that are valued at or under $800 and that would otherwise qualify for the de minimis exemption will be subject to a duty rate of either 30% of their value or $25 per item. This will increase to $50 per item after June 1. This is in lieu of any other duties, including those imposed by prior orders.

The order is intended to not only address the proliferation of packages being sent to the U.S. without paying appropriate duties, but also deceptive shipping practices that have allowed the proliferation of synthetic opioids into the U.S.

PDD (NASDAQ:PDD), Alibaba (BABA), and JD.com (JD) shares were all down ~5%.

data.title: U.S. stock futures veer lower after hours as Trump outlines reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
U.S. stock index futures flipped lower in Wednesday's after-hours trading, as President Donald Trump outlined import tariffs on products coming worldwide into the U.S.

The minimum baseline tariff will be 10% and 49% is the highest tariff rate. Trump spoke at the White House's Rose Garden after day-session trading closed. As pre-announced, the U.S. will impose a 25% tariff on all foreign-made automobiles as of midnight.

Benchmark S&P 500 futures (SPX) fell -1.7%, and Nasdaq-100 futures (US100:IND) were -2.5%. Dow futures (INDU) shed -0.5%. Futures had popped up more than 1% as Trump's afternoon press event got underway.

Among the tariff plans, the U.S. will place a 34% levy rate on goods from China, a 24% rate for Japanese goods, a 20% rate on European Union products, and a 10% levy on U.K. and Brazilian products. 

Widely watched exchange-traded funds tracking key market indexes fell late Wednesday. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) -3.3%, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) -2.6%, and the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:VOO) -2.5%.

Canada and Mexico were absent from the White House's list of reciprocal tariffs. The U.S. dollar fell -0.9% against the Mexico's currency (USD:MXN), buying 20.172 pesos. The greenback also fell against Canada's currency (USD:CAD), down -0.5%. The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) -0.5%. 

Stocks closed the regular-session higher after spending most of the day in positive territory.

In the bond market, the the 10-year yield (US10Y) fell 5 basis points to 4.12%, while the shorter-end 2-year yield (US2Y) lost 5 basis points to 3.84%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Bank stocks slide after tariffs loom over deal prospects
data.description:
Bank stocks dipped in Wednesday after-hours trading after President Donald Trump announced reciprocal tariffs against other countries that range from 10% to 49%.

While Trump said he expects that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices, in the short-term it's expected to increase prices for most imported goods. That could fuel inflation, which could lead the Federal Reserve to raise interest rates. As a result, higher interest rates would constrain M&A activity, as well as debt and equity issuance, services that bring in fees for banks.

JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) stock fell 2.4%, Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) stock slid 2.6%, Morgan Stanley (NYSE:MS) fell 2.7%, Citigroup (NYSE:C) dipped 2.5%, Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) declined 2.3%, and Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) wilted 3.6%.

Large, regional bank stocks also shared in the pain. U.S. Bancorp (USB) -2.3%, Truist Financial (TFC) -1.8%, PNC Financial Services (PNC) -1.1%, Flagstar Financial (FLG) -2.0%, and Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) -2.7%.

Consumer finance stocks sank as well. Capital One Financial (COF) -3.3%, American Express (AXP) -2.1%, Synchrony Financial (SYF) -5.5%, Bread Financial Holdings (BFH) -3.1%, and Discover Financial Services (DFS) -4.0%. Auto lender Ally Financial (ALLY) dropped 1.6%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Owens & Minor announces upsize and pricing of senior secured notes offering
data.description:
• Owens & Minor (NYSE:OMI) on Wednesday announced the upsize and pricing of its private offering of $1B aggregate principal amount of its 10.000% senior secured notes due 2030.
  • The offering is expected to close on April 4, 2025.
  • The notes will bear interest at the rate of 10.000% per year and will mature on April 15, 2030.
  • The notes were offered at a price of 100% of their principal amount.
  • Unless the previously announced acquisition of Rotech Healthcare Holdings is consummated substantially concurrently with the closing of the offering, the company will deposit the gross proceeds from the offering into a segregated escrow account until the date that the acquisition has been consummated.
  • OMI -3.38% after hours to $8.85.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Newsmax stock loses steam and gives up gains from post-IPO excitement
data.description:
Shares of conservative network Newsmax (NYSE:NMAX) ended Wednesday's trading session with a 77.5% decline, after rallying in triple-digit percentages since IPOing this week.

NMAX stock closed up 735.1% at $83.51 on Monday and soared 179% to close at $233 on Tuesday, causing its market cap to touch nearly $30B and making it more valuable than Fox (NASDAQ:FOXA) for a brief period.

With today's decline, the stock gave up a majority of its gains from its stellar yet volatile two-day rally, losing $180.48 per share during Wednesday's open market trading, and its valuation came down to just under $5B.

More than 10M shares changed hands by the end of the trading session, 1.7x its three-month daily moving average.

Four out of five SA analysts covering the stock rate it "Sell"; one has rated it "Hold."

Seeking Alpha contributor Mike Fay wrote in his article on Wednesday that Newsmax is a meme stock but is being priced like a growth stock because of "admittedly impressive top-line results."

He notes that the company's strongest revenue growth is in a business segment that is reliant on pay-TV subscribers, which he said "is a consumer cohort that has been in secular decline for over a decade."

Fay also listed out that the company's negative digital revenue growth in 2024, CEO Chris Ruddy's 81.4% voting power, and the litigation surrounding the network are among the reasons for him to rate the stock a "Strong Sell."

"I wouldn't walk; I'd run away from NMAX shares," Fay concluded.

data.title: H&R Block names Richard A. Johnson chairman
data.description:
H&R Block (NYSE:HRB) named Richard A. Johnson as chairman, effective April 1, 2025, in line with the tax preparer company's board refreshment and succession planning, it said on Wednesday.

Johnson, who has been on the company's board since 2015, succeeds Robert A. Gerard, who has served as chairman since 2011.

"The time has come for Dick to take over board leadership as we transition to our next 5-year strategy for growth and transformation," Gerard said.

Johnson spent much of his career at Foot Locker, having served as president, CEO, and chairman. He also serves on the boards of Build-A-Bear Workshop (BBW) and Graebel Companies.

H&R Block (NYSE:HRB) stock slipped 1.0% in Wednesday after-hours trading.

data.title: Boeing CEO tells Senate: Focus is on safety, not speed, after 737 Max crisis
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) Chief Executive Kelly Ortberg assured U.S. lawmakers Wednesday that the aerospace giant is prioritizing safety over production speed as it works to recover from a series of high-profile incidents, including a mid-air emergency involving a 737 Max 9 aircraft in early 2024.

Speaking before the Senate Commerce Committee in his first appearance since taking over the top job last August, Ortberg said Boeing (NYSE:BA) implemented “sweeping changes” aimed at improving quality control, rebuilding trust, and preventing future safety failures.

“I’m not pressuring the team to go fast. I’m pressuring the team to do it right,” Ortberg told senators. He noted that while the company hopes to ramp up production of the 737 MAX to more than 38 aircraft a month later this year, it is not committing to a specific timeline.

Safety culture under scrutiny

Ortberg’s testimony came amid intensified scrutiny from lawmakers and regulators following a January 2024 incident in which a door panel on an Alaska Airlines 737 Max 9 detached mid-flight. The near-disaster reignited concerns about Boeing’s (BA) safety culture and led to a temporary cap by the Federal Aviation Administration on 737 Max production at 38 planes a month.

Since the incident, Ortberg said Boeing (BA) overhauled its internal processes, expanded inspections across its facilities and at key suppliers and improved how components — such as fuselage door plugs — are handled during assembly. The company also mandated additional checks on every fuselage door to prevent future defects.

The Jan. 5 incident was a “cathartic moment for all of us,” Ortberg said. “It’s critically important that we rebuild trust.”

Measurable progress, but challenges remain

Ortberg pointed to early signs of progress under his leadership. The amount of out-of-sequence work — known in the industry as "traveled work" — has dropped by 50%, while defects from Spirit AeroSystems (SPR), Boeing’s (BA) fuselage supplier, have fallen by 56%. Boeing (BA) is in the process of acquiring Spirit to gain tighter control over its production standards.

The CEO also emphasized that Boeing’s (BA) recent strategy includes withholding aggressive financial guidance to avoid repeating past mistakes that placed undue pressure on workers. This approach contrasts sharply with previous leadership, which was criticized for prioritizing financial targets at the expense of safety.

“They know we’ve got to do this right,” Ortberg said of airline customers frustrated by delivery delays.

Lawmakers press for accountability

While Ortberg’s appearance was met with a more measured tone than past hearings involving his predecessor, lawmakers still voiced concern about the company’s oversight failures. Senate Commerce Committee Chair Ted Cruz criticized Boeing’s (BA) prior lack of internal auditing and oversight of third-party suppliers, which he said contributed to a “lack of safety culture.”

Ortberg acknowledged the company’s past failings, admitting that Boeing (BA) had made “serious missteps” in recent years but has since taken action to course-correct.

The hearing also took place against the backdrop of ongoing legal troubles. Boeing (BA) is currently awaiting a trial this June over its plea agreement related to the two fatal 737 Max crashes in 2018 and 2019. Last year, the company agreed to plead guilty to a criminal fraud charge and pay over $240 million after breaching a previous settlement.

Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy recently said Boeing (BA) had “lost the trust of the American people,” while the former FAA chief warned that restoring a strong safety culture could take years.

Ortberg, however, struck an optimistic tone, saying he was confident in the progress being made—even as he reiterated his commitment to safety over speed.

“I suspect [higher production rates] will be sometime this year,” he said.

data.title: Boeing awarded $133.5M U.S. Navy contract modification
data.description:
• Boeing (NYSE:BA) on Wednesday is awarded a $133.5 million firm-fixed-price, cost-plus-fixed-fee modification for adding scope to procure P-8A training systems phase two software development, integration and device installation for the government of South Korea.
  • The modification is to include one operational flight trainer, one weapon tactics trainer, one maintenance electronic classroom, and one training system support center as well as associated electronic classroom instructor led training.
  • Work will be performed in St. Louis, Missouri (80%); and Pohang, South Korea (20%), and is expected to be completed in October 2028.
  • Naval Air Warfare Center Training Systems Division, Orlando, Florida, is the contracting activity.

data.title: Trump's 'Liberation Day' tariff announcement sends U.S. Treasury Yields lower
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields fell on Wednesday evening following a major announcement by President Donald Trump regarding trade tariffs. The President declared that, in a significant shift, he would be imposing reciprocal tariffs, a move he described as marking a "Liberation Day."

The announcement, which sent waves through financial markets, comes amid ongoing discussions about the impact of trade policies on the broader economy. Traders reacted by moving into bonds, pushing yields lower in response to the uncertainty surrounding the new tariff measures. 

The popular benchmark U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) has come down 3 basis points down to 4.13% as it pared back from its earlier day high of 4.24%. 

At the same time a similar story has played out for the U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) which is lower by 1 basis point to 3.86% as it retraced from its intraday high of 3.96%. 

See how yields trade across the entire curve here on Seeking Alpha’s bond page. 

Here are some popular exchange-traded funds linked to the fixed-income market: 

Treasury ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), and (NYSEARCA:BIL). 

Fixed Income ETFs: (NYSEARCA:AGG), (NASDAQ:BND), (NASDAQ:VCIT), (NYSEARCA:MUB), (NASDAQ:MBB), (NYSEARCA:JNK), (NYSEARCA:LQD), (NYSEARCA:HYG), and (NYSEARCA:TIP).

data.title: Major restaurant chains warn about disruption and higher pricing from reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
The National Restaurant Association warned about the impact of new reciprocal tariffs on the restaurant industry.

CEO Michelle Korsmo said applying new tariffs at the new scale will create change and disruption that restaurant operators will have to navigate to keep their restaurants open.

"The biggest concerns for restaurant operators—from community restaurants to national brands—are that tariffs will hike food and packaging costs and add uncertainty to managing availability, while pushing prices up for consumers."

Korsmo also noted that restaurant operators rely on a stable supply of fresh ingredients year-round to provide the menu items their customers want and expect. "Many restaurant operators source as many domestic ingredients as they can, but it’s simply not possible for U.S. farmers and ranchers to produce the volumes needed to support consumer demand," she added.

The National Restaurant Association said it will continue to share with the White House the real-life challenges these changes present for restaurant operators and ask to have food and beverages exempted from these tariffs.

McDonald's (NYSE:MCD), Yum! Brands (YUM), Darden Restaurants (DRI), Chipotle C(MG), Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), Bloomin' Brands (BLMN), and Brinker International (EAT) are just a few of the companies that are members of the industry association.
